text:
AI-native system design is the practice of architecting software systems where AI components
(LLMs, embedding models, vector databases, agents) are first-class citizens of the architecture
— not bolted-on features, but core structural elements that the entire system is designed around.

Traditional system design adds AI as a feature.
AI-native system design builds the system around AI capabilities.

Key difference:
- Traditional: "We have a web app, let's add a chatbot feature"
- AI-native: "We are building an intelligent system — LLM reasoning, retrieval, and agents
  are the core, everything else (API, DB, queues) supports them"

AI-native systems require EXTENSIONS to standard HLD thinking:

Standard HLD components still apply:
→ Load Balancer, API Gateway, Caching, Message Queues, Databases, CDN

AI-native additions:
→ LLM Inference Layer
→ Embedding Pipeline
→ Vector Database
→ RAG Architecture
→ Agentic Design
→ Multi-Agent Orchestration
→ AI Observability (Langfuse, tracing)
→ Human-in-the-Loop gates
→ Prompt Management Layer
→ Semantic Caching

Six core AI-native patterns (2024-2026 era):
1. RAG Architecture — Retrieval Augmented Generation
2. Agentic Design — ReAct, Reflection, Planning patterns
3. Multi-Agent Systems — Orchestrator + specialist agents
4. LLM Inference Layer — model routing, hosting, optimization
5. AI Observability — tracing, monitoring, evaluation
6. Human-in-the-Loop — approval gates, oversight, intervention

MASTER DECISION RULE FOR AI-NATIVE PROJECTS:
- "chatbot with custom knowledge" → RAG Architecture
- "AI that takes actions / uses tools" → Agentic Design
- "complex AI workflow with multiple AI roles" → Multi-Agent System
- "need to switch models, optimize cost" → LLM Inference Layer (LiteLLM)
- "need to monitor AI quality and cost" → AI Observability (Langfuse)
- "AI making critical decisions" → Human-in-the-Loop mandatory

DEFINITION:
RAG is an AI architecture pattern that enhances LLM responses by retrieving relevant context
from an external knowledge base before generating a response. Instead of relying solely on
the LLM's training data, RAG grounds responses in specific, up-to-date, custom knowledge.

Core principle: Retrieve relevant context → augment LLM prompt → generate grounded response.

WHY RAG EXISTS:
Problems with pure LLM:
- Knowledge cutoff — LLM doesn't know recent information
- Hallucination — LLM makes up answers when it doesn't know
- No custom knowledge — LLM doesn't know your company's data
- Context window limits — can't feed entire knowledge base into prompt

RAG solves all of these by retrieving only the relevant chunks at query time.

RAG FULL ARCHITECTURE:

INGESTION PIPELINE (offline, runs once or on update):
[Raw Documents (PDF, DOCX, TXT, HTML, CSV)]
       ↓
[Document Loader] — loads documents into memory
       ↓
[Text Splitter / Chunker] — splits into smaller chunks
       ↓
[Embedding Model] — converts each chunk into a vector (numerical representation)
       ↓
[Vector Database] — stores chunks + their vectors + metadata
       ↓
[Ingestion Complete]

RETRIEVAL + GENERATION PIPELINE (online, runs per user query):
[User Query]
       ↓
[Query Embedding] — embed the query using same embedding model
       ↓
[Vector Similarity Search] — find top-K most similar chunks in vector DB
       ↓
[Reranker (optional)] — reorder retrieved chunks by relevance
       ↓
[Prompt Construction] — system prompt + retrieved chunks + user query
       ↓
[LLM] — generates response grounded in retrieved context
       ↓
[Response to User]

DETAILED COMPONENT BREAKDOWN:

1. DOCUMENT LOADERS:
   - PDF: PyPDF2, pdfplumber, Unstructured
   - DOCX: python-docx, Unstructured
   - HTML: BeautifulSoup, Unstructured
   - CSV/Excel: pandas
   - Web scraping: Playwright, Scrapy
   - LangChain provides unified loaders for all formats
   - Decision: Use Unstructured for mixed document types, specific loaders for known formats

2. TEXT SPLITTING / CHUNKING:
   Chunking strategy is one of the most critical RAG decisions.
   Bad chunking = bad retrieval = bad answers regardless of LLM quality.

apy
   - LangChain provides unified loaders for all formats
   - Decision: Use Unstructured for mixed document types, specific loaders for known formats

2. TEXT SPLITTING / CHUNKING:
   Chunking strategy is one of the most critical RAG decisions.
   Bad chunking = bad retrieval = bad answers regardless of LLM quality.

   Chunking strategies:
   a) Fixed Size Chunking:
      - Split every N characters or tokens
      - Simple, consistent chunk sizes
      - Cons: may cut sentences mid-way
      - Use when: uniform documents, simple use cases
      - Typical size: 512-1024 tokens with 50-100 token overlap

   b) Recursive Character Splitting:
      - Split by paragraph → sentence → word in order of preference
      - Preserves semantic boundaries better than fixed size
      - LangChain RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter — most common choice
      - Use when: general text documents

   c) Semantic Chunking:
      - Use embedding similarity to detect topic boundaries
      - Split where semantic meaning changes
      - Higher quality chunks, more expensive to compute
      - Use when: high-quality retrieval is critical

   d) Document Structure Chunking:
      - Use document headings, sections as chunk boundaries
      - Best for: structured documents (legal, technical, manuals)
      - Preserves document hierarchy

   e) Sentence Window Chunking:
      - Store individual sentences but retrieve surrounding sentences as context
      - Good retrieval precision with good context
      - Use when: fine-grained retrieval with contextual window

for: structured documents (legal, technical, manuals)
      - Preserves document hierarchy

   e) Sentence Window Chunking:
      - Store individual sentences but retrieve surrounding sentences as context
      - Good retrieval precision with good context
      - Use when: fine-grained retrieval with contextual window

   CHUNK SIZE DECISION RULE:
   - Short factual docs (FAQs) → small chunks (256-512 tokens)
   - Long technical docs → medium chunks (512-1024 tokens)
   - Legal/compliance docs → structure-based chunking
   - Conversational context → sentence-level chunking
   - Always add chunk overlap (10-20% of chunk size) to avoid cutting context

   METADATA TO STORE WITH EACH CHUNK:
   - source (filename, URL)
   - page_number
   - section_title
   - document_type
   - created_at / updated_at
   - chunk_index
   - custom tags (category, domain, language)
   Metadata enables filtered retrieval — search only relevant document types.

3. EMBEDDING MODELS:
   An embedding model converts text into a dense vector (array of numbers) that captures
   semantic meaning. Similar texts have vectors that are close in vector space.

   Popular embedding models:
   - OpenAI text-embedding-3-small — best balance of quality and cost, 1536 dimensions
   - OpenAI text-embedding-3-large — highest quality, 3072 dimensions, higher cost
   - sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2 — open source, fast, 384 dimensions
   - BAAI/bge-large-en-v1.5 — high quality open source
   - Cohere Embed v3 — strong multilingual support
   - Nomic Embed — open source, 768 dimensions, strong performance

t-embedding-3-large — highest quality, 3072 dimensions, higher cost
   - sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2 — open source, fast, 384 dimensions
   - BAAI/bge-large-en-v1.5 — high quality open source
   - Cohere Embed v3 — strong multilingual support
   - Nomic Embed — open source, 768 dimensions, strong performance

   Decision rules:
   - Cost-sensitive production → text-embedding-3-small (OpenAI)
   - Open source / on-premise → BAAI/bge-large or nomic-embed-text
   - Multilingual → Cohere Embed v3
   - Fast local embedding → all-MiniLM-L6-v2
   - Always use the SAME embedding model for ingestion and retrieval

4. VECTOR DATABASES:
   A vector database stores embeddings and enables fast similarity search using
   Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) algorithms.

   Popular vector databases:
   - Qdrant — open source, Rust-based, high performance, rich filtering, self-hostable
   - Pinecone — fully managed, easy to use, expensive at scale
   - Weaviate — open source, GraphQL API, built-in vectorization
   - Chroma — lightweight, in-process, great for development
   - PGVector — PostgreSQL extension, vector search inside existing Postgres
   - Milvus — open source, high scale, production-grade
   - FAISS — Facebook, in-memory, no persistence, great for prototyping

   Decision rules:
   - Production, self-hosted, high performance → Qdrant (your current choice, correct)
   - Simple prototype / development → Chroma
   - Already using PostgreSQL, small scale → PGVector
   - Fully managed, no DevOps → Pinecone
   - Billion-scale vectors → Milvus

   Similarity search types:
   - Cosine Similarity — measures angle between vectors (most common, use for normalized vectors)
   - Dot Product — measures magnitude and angle (use for non-normalized)
   - Euclidean Distance (L2) — geometric distance between vectors

5. RETRIEVAL STRATEGIES:
   a) Dense Retrieval (standard):
      - Query embedded → similarity search in vector DB → top-K chunks returned
      - Fast, good semantic understanding
      - Weakness: may miss exact keyword matches

   b) Sparse Retrieval (BM25):
      - Traditional keyword-based search (TF-IDF, BM25)
      - Good for exact keyword matches
      - Weakness: no semantic understanding

   c) Hybrid Retrieval (best):
      - Combine dense + sparse retrieval
      - Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) to merge results
      - Best of both worlds — semantic + keyword matching
      - Qdrant supports hybrid search natively
      - Use when: production RAG where retrieval quality is critical

   d) Multi-Query Retrieval:
      - LLM generates multiple versions of the user query
      - Run retrieval for each version
      - Merge and deduplicate results
      - Use when: user queries are ambiguous or short

supports hybrid search natively
      - Use when: production RAG where retrieval quality is critical

   d) Multi-Query Retrieval:
      - LLM generates multiple versions of the user query
      - Run retrieval for each version
      - Merge and deduplicate results
      - Use when: user queries are ambiguous or short

   e) Contextual Compression:
      - Retrieved chunks compressed/filtered to extract only relevant parts
      - Reduces token usage in LLM context
      - Use when: chunks are large and partially relevant

6. RERANKING:
   After initial retrieval (top-20), a reranker re-scores and reorders results to find
   the truly most relevant chunks (top-5).

   Why: Vector similarity is good but not perfect. Reranker adds a second pass of
   relevance scoring using a cross-encoder model.

   Tools: Cohere Rerank, BGE Reranker, FlashRank (local, free)
   Pattern: Retrieve top-20 with vector search → rerank to top-5 → send to LLM
   Decision rule: Any production RAG system → add reranking for better quality

7. PROMPT CONSTRUCTION FOR RAG:
   System prompt structure:
   - Role definition
   - Instruction to use only provided context
   - Instruction to say "I don't know" if context doesn't contain answer
   - Formatting instructions
   - Context injection (retrieved chunks)
   - User query

   Example structure:
   """
   You are [role]. Answer questions based ONLY on the provided context.
   If the context does not contain the answer, say "I don't know based on available information."
   Do not make up information.

   Context:
   {retrieved_chunks}

RAG EVALUATION METRICS:
- Retrieval Recall — did we retrieve all relevant chunks?
- Retrieval Precision — are all retrieved chunks relevant?
- Answer Faithfulness — is the answer grounded in retrieved context?
- Answer Relevance — does the answer address the question?
- Context Relevance — is the retrieved context relevant to the question?
Tools: RAGAS framework for automated RAG evaluation

ADVANCED RAG PATTERNS:
- Self-RAG: LLM decides when to retrieve and evaluates its own outputs
- Corrective RAG (CRAG): If retrieved docs are irrelevant, fall back to web search
- GraphRAG: Build knowledge graph from documents, traverse graph for retrieval
- Agentic RAG: Agent decides retrieval strategy dynamically based on query type

DECISION RULE — WHEN TO USE RAG:
- "chatbot that answers from company documents" → RAG
- "document QA system" → RAG
- "knowledge base assistant" → RAG
- "AI that needs up-to-date information" → RAG + periodic reingestion
- "legal/compliance document analysis" → RAG with structure-based chunking
- "AI with general world knowledge only" → pure LLM, no RAG needed
- "AI coding assistant" → RAG over codebase

DEFINITION:
An AI agent is a system that uses an LLM as its reasoning engine to autonomously decide
what actions to take, execute those actions using tools, observe results, and repeat until
the goal is achieved. Agents go beyond single LLM calls — they plan, act, and adapt.

Core principle: LLM reasons → selects tool → executes tool → observes result → reasons again → repeat.

WHY AGENTS EXIST:
Single LLM call limitations:
- Can only answer in one shot — no ability to take multi-step actions
- Cannot interact with external systems (APIs, databases, files)
- Cannot adapt based on intermediate results
- Cannot handle complex multi-step workflows

Agents solve this with:
- Tool use — LLM can call functions, APIs, search engines
- Memory — remember what happened in previous steps
- Planning — decompose complex goals into sub-tasks
- Reflection — evaluate own outputs and self-correct

AGENTIC DESIGN PATTERNS:

1. ReAct Pattern (Reasoning + Acting) — most fundamental:
   Loop: [Thought] → [Action] → [Observation] → [Thought] → [Action] → [Observation] → [Final Answer]

   - Thought: LLM reasons about what to do next
   - Action: LLM calls a tool with specific inputs
   - Observation: Tool returns result, LLM reads it
   - Repeats until goal achieved or max iterations reached

mental:
   Loop: [Thought] → [Action] → [Observation] → [Thought] → [Action] → [Observation] → [Final Answer]

   - Thought: LLM reasons about what to do next
   - Action: LLM calls a tool with specific inputs
   - Observation: Tool returns result, LLM reads it
   - Repeats until goal achieved or max iterations reached

   Use when: Agent needs to use tools to answer questions or complete tasks
   Example: User asks "What's the weather in Surat and should I carry an umbrella?"
   → Thought: I need weather data for Surat
   → Action: call weather_tool(city="Surat")
   → Observation: "Rainy, 28°C, 80% precipitation"
   → Thought: User should carry umbrella
   → Final Answer: "It's rainy in Surat today, definitely carry an umbrella"

2. Reflection Pattern:
   - Agent generates output → critiques its own output → revises → repeat
   - Improves output quality through self-evaluation
   - Phases: Generate → Reflect → Revise → (repeat N times) → Output

   Use when: Output quality is critical, first draft may be imperfect
   Example: Code generation agent — writes code → checks for bugs → fixes → outputs

3. Planning Pattern (Plan-and-Execute):
   - Planner LLM creates a step-by-step plan
   - Executor LLM executes each step one at a time
   - Two separate LLM calls: one for planning, one for execution
   - Separation allows using cheaper model for execution, expensive for planning

   Use when: Complex multi-step tasks where upfront planning improves execution
   Example: Research agent — plan steps to research a topic → execute each step

4. Self-Ask Pattern:
   - Agent breaks complex question into sub-questions
   - Answers each sub-question using tools
   - Combines answers to answer the original question
   Use when: Complex questions requiring multiple information lookups

5. Chain-of-Thought (CoT) with Tools:
   - Agent reasons step by step before acting
   - Extended thinking before tool calls
   - Improves accuracy on complex reasoning tasks

AGENT COMPONENTS:

1. LLM (Brain): The reasoning engine. Decides what to do, interprets results.
2. Tools: Functions the agent can call. Must have: name, description, input schema.
3. Memory: What the agent remembers.
4. Orchestrator: Controls the agent loop — when to stop, max iterations, error handling.

TOOL DESIGN (critical for agent quality):
Each tool must have:
- name: clear, descriptive (snake_case)
- description: precise explanation of what the tool does and when to use it
  (LLM reads this to decide whether to call the tool)
- input_schema: JSON Schema defining required and optional parameters
- output_format: what the tool returns (LLM must understand the output)

Tool description quality directly determines agent intelligence.
Bad description → wrong tool selection → wrong actions → wrong results.

AGENT MEMORY TYPES:

1. In-Context Memory (short-term):
   - Conversation history stored in LLM context window
   - Lost after conversation ends
   - Limited by context window size
   - Use for: current conversation tracking

2. External Memory (long-term):
   - Store conversation summaries or key facts in DB or vector store
   - Retrieved at start of new conversation
   - Use for: personalization, remembering user preferences across sessions
   - Implementation: Redis (key-value), PostgreSQL (structured), Vector DB (semantic search)

3. Episodic Memory:
   - Remember specific past interactions and their outcomes
   - Use for: learning from past agent actions, avoiding repeated mistakes

AGENT STOPPING CONDITIONS:
- Task completed successfully
- Max iterations reached (prevent infinite loops)
- LLM returns final answer signal
- Error threshold exceeded
- Human intervention requested

LANGGRAPH FOR AGENTIC DESIGN (your stack):
- LangGraph models agent logic as a directed graph (nodes + edges)
- Nodes: individual processing steps (LLM call, tool call, condition check)
- Edges: transitions between steps (conditional or unconditional)
- State: shared data object passed between all nodes
- Supports: cycles (loops), branching, parallel execution, human-in-the-loop
- Best for: complex multi-step agentic workflows with conditional logic

LangGraph state design:
- Define TypedDict for state (messages, context, tool_results, iteration_count)
- Each node reads from state and writes back to state
- Conditional edges route based on state values (e.g., "is task complete?")

DECISION RULE — WHEN TO USE AGENTIC DESIGN:
- "AI that browses the web" → agent with web search tool
- "AI that writes and executes code" → coding agent
- "AI that manages tasks in external systems" → agent with API tools
- "AI that answers complex multi-step questions" → ReAct agent
- "AI that generates and self-improves content" → Reflection pattern
- "AI personal assistant" → agent with calendar, email, task tools
- "simple Q&A chatbot" → RAG only, no agent needed
- "document summarization" → single LLM call, no agent needed

DEFINITION:
A multi-agent system is an architecture where multiple AI agents, each with specialized
roles and capabilities, collaborate to accomplish complex tasks that exceed what a single
agent can reliably handle.

Core principle: Divide complex tasks among specialist agents → orchestrate collaboration → achieve results
no single agent could achieve alone.

WHY MULTI-AGENT:
Single agent limitations:
- Context window overflow on very large tasks
- One agent trying to be expert in everything = mediocre at everything
- Serial processing — can't parallelize independent sub-tasks
- Harder to maintain and debug a monolithic agent

Multi-agent advantages:
- Specialization — each agent is expert in its domain
- Parallelism — independent agents work simultaneously
- Modularity — add/remove agents without redesigning the system
- Better quality — specialist agents outperform generalist agents

MULTI-AGENT PATTERNS:

1. Orchestrator-Specialist Pattern (most common):
   Structure:
   [User Request] → [Orchestrator Agent] → routes to → [Specialist Agent 1]
                                        → routes to → [Specialist Agent 2]
                                        → routes to → [Specialist Agent 3]
                                        ← collects results ←
                    [Orchestrator Agent] → [Final Response to User]

   Orchestrator responsibilities:
   - Understand overall task
   - Decompose into sub-tasks
   - Route sub-tasks to appropriate specialist agents
   - Collect and synthesize results
   - Handle errors and retries

← collects results ←
                    [Orchestrator Agent] → [Final Response to User]

   Orchestrator responsibilities:
   - Understand overall task
   - Decompose into sub-tasks
   - Route sub-tasks to appropriate specialist agents
   - Collect and synthesize results
   - Handle errors and retries

   Specialist agent responsibilities:
   - Execute one specific type of task very well
   - Return structured results to orchestrator

   Example (your AIMaven agents):
   Orchestrator → Plan & Architect Agent (HLD/LLD planning)
              → Schema & DB Agent (database design)
              → Code Generation Agent (feature coding)
              → Fix Issue Agent (bug fixing)
              → Auth & Security Agent (auth implementation)

2. Supervisor Pattern:
   - Supervisor agent monitors other agents' work
   - Evaluates output quality
   - Decides if work needs to be redone
   - Can reassign tasks if an agent fails

3. Peer-to-Peer (Collaborative) Pattern:
   - Agents communicate directly with each other (no central orchestrator)
   - Each agent can call other agents as tools
   - More flexible but harder to control and debug
   - Use when: agents have equal authority and need dynamic collaboration

4. Sequential Pipeline Pattern:
   - Output of Agent 1 → Input of Agent 2 → Input of Agent 3
   - Each agent transforms or enriches the data
   - Simple, predictable, easy to debug
   - Use when: task has clear sequential steps

   Example:
   [Requirements Agent] → [Architecture Agent] → [Schema Agent] → [Code Agent]

4. Sequential Pipeline Pattern:
   - Output of Agent 1 → Input of Agent 2 → Input of Agent 3
   - Each agent transforms or enriches the data
   - Simple, predictable, easy to debug
   - Use when: task has clear sequential steps

   Example:
   [Requirements Agent] → [Architecture Agent] → [Schema Agent] → [Code Agent]

5. Parallel Execution Pattern:
   - Orchestrator sends same task to multiple agents simultaneously
   - Results merged (voting, synthesis, best-of-N selection)
   - Use when: tasks are independent and can run concurrently
   - Use when: need diverse perspectives or redundancy

AGENT COMMUNICATION:
- Shared State: all agents read/write to a central state object (LangGraph approach)
- Message Passing: agents send structured messages to each other
- Blackboard: shared memory space all agents read from and write to
- Direct Tool Calls: one agent calls another agent as a tool

INTER-AGENT PROTOCOL:
Each agent should have a defined interface:
- Input schema: what it accepts
- Output schema: what it returns
- Error handling: what it returns on failure
- Timeout: maximum execution time

- Shared memory: all agents access common state (LangGraph state)
- Long-term storage: results persisted to DB for cross-session memory
- Vector memory: past agent actions stored as embeddings for retrieval

DECISION RULE — WHEN TO USE MULTI-AGENT:
- "complex AI system with 3+ distinct AI roles" → Multi-Agent
- "tasks that can be parallelized" → Multi-Agent with parallel pattern
- "single agent context overflow" → decompose into multi-agent

DEFINITION:
The LLM inference layer is the architectural component that manages all interactions with
LLM models — routing requests to the right model, optimizing costs, handling failures,
caching responses, and abstracting model provider differences.

Core principle: Decouple application logic from specific LLM providers → flexibility, cost control, reliability.

WHY A DEDICATED INFERENCE LAYER:
Without it:
- Application code directly calls OpenAI API → vendor lock-in
- No fallback if OpenAI is down
- No cost optimization (always using expensive models)
- No request routing (always using same model for all tasks)
- No semantic caching

With inference layer:
- Switch models without changing application code
- Route simple tasks to cheap models, complex tasks to powerful models
- Fallback to alternative provider if primary is down
- Semantic caching to avoid redundant LLM calls
- Unified logging and cost tracking

LITELLM — THE STANDARD INFERENCE LAYER (your stack):
LiteLLM provides a unified API interface for 100+ LLM providers.
- Call OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Mistral, Ollama with the same code
- Automatic fallback — if primary model fails, try backup model
- Load balancing across multiple API keys or model deployments
- Cost tracking per request
- Async support

LiteLLM usage:
completion(model="gpt-4o", messages=[...])
completion(model="anthropic/claude-3-5-sonnet", messages=[...])
completion(model="ollama/llama3", messages=[...])
Same interface, different models.

MODEL ROUTING STRATEGIES:

1. Task-Based Routing:
   - Route based on task complexity or type
   - Simple tasks (classification, extraction) → cheap fast model (GPT-4o-mini, Haiku)
   - Complex tasks (planning, reasoning) → powerful model (GPT-4o, Claude Sonnet)
   - Code generation → code-specialized model (Claude Sonnet, GPT-4o)
   Decision: Define task categories and map to models in config

2. Cost-Based Routing:
   - Monitor token usage per request
   - Route to cheaper model when within budget
   - Escalate to expensive model only when needed

3. Latency-Based Routing:
   - Route to fastest available model when latency is critical
   - Route to more powerful model when quality matters more than speed

4. Fallback Routing:
   - Primary model → if fails → secondary model → if fails → tertiary
   - Example: GPT-4o → Claude Sonnet → Gemini Pro
   - LiteLLM handles this automatically with fallbacks config

LLM INFERENCE OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES:

1. Semantic Caching:
   - Cache LLM responses for semantically similar queries
   - Not exact match — if new query means the same thing, return cached response
   - Store: {query_embedding, response} in Redis or vector DB
   - Similarity threshold (e.g., cosine > 0.95) → cache hit
   - Saves significant cost for repeated/similar queries

2. Prompt Caching:
   - Cache the processed prompt prefix at the model provider level
   - Anthropic Claude supports prompt caching — cache system prompt
   - Reduces input token cost for repeated system prompts (up to 90% reduction)
   - Use when: long system prompt used in every request

3. Batching:
   - Combine multiple requests into one API call
   - Reduces API overhead
   - Use for: batch processing of documents, async workloads

4. Streaming:
   - Stream LLM response token by token
   - Improves perceived latency (user sees response appearing)
   - Use for: any user-facing chat interface
   - Implementation: Server-Sent Events (SSE) from FastAPI

5. Token Optimization:
   - Minimize prompt length without losing quality
   - Remove redundant instructions
   - Use shorter few-shot examples
   - Compress context (summarize conversation history)

6. Context Window Management:
   - Sliding window: keep only last N messages in context
   - Summarization: summarize older messages, keep summary in context
   - Selective retrieval: only include relevant past context

DEFINITION:
AI observability is the practice of monitoring, tracing, and evaluating AI system behavior —
including LLM inputs/outputs, token usage, latency, costs, retrieval quality, and agent
decision paths — to understand, debug, and improve AI systems in production.

Core principle: You cannot improve what you cannot measure. AI systems need specialized
observability beyond traditional APM tools.

WHY AI OBSERVABILITY IS DIFFERENT:
Traditional observability (logs, metrics, traces) is not enough for AI because:
- LLM responses are probabilistic — same input can give different outputs
- Quality is subjective — need to evaluate if response was actually good
- Token costs accumulate — need per-request cost tracking
- Agent reasoning paths need to be traced across multiple steps
- Retrieval quality in RAG needs separate evaluation
- Prompt changes have non-obvious effects on output quality

AI OBSERVABILITY COMPONENTS:

1. LLM Tracing:
   - Capture every LLM call: input prompt, output, model, tokens, latency, cost
   - Trace multi-step agent execution end-to-end
   - Visualize the full reasoning chain of an agent
   - Debug which step failed in a multi-agent pipeline

2. Token Usage Tracking:
   - Track input tokens, output tokens, total tokens per request
   - Calculate cost per request, per user, per feature
   - Set budget alerts when costs exceed thresholds
   - Identify expensive prompts to optimize

3. Latency Monitoring:

4. Quality Evaluation:

6. Dataset Management:

   - Run automated evaluation against golden dataset on every prompt change

DEFINITION:
Human-in-the-Loop is a design pattern where human oversight, review, and approval is
integrated at specific points in an AI workflow — particularly at decision points where
autonomous AI action could have significant consequences.

Core principle: AI does the heavy lifting, humans maintain control over critical decisions.

WHY HUMAN-IN-THE-LOOP:
AI systems can:
- Hallucinate — generate confidently wrong information
- Make decisions with unintended consequences
- Misinterpret ambiguous instructions
- Act outside intended boundaries

Human-in-the-loop prevents:
- AI autonomously taking irreversible harmful actions
- High-stakes decisions made without human review
- AI drift outside intended behavior over time
- Loss of user/stakeholder trust

TYPES OF HUMAN INTERVENTION:

1. Approval Gates:
   - AI proposes an action → human reviews → approves or rejects → AI proceeds or stops
   - Synchronous: AI waits for human decision before continuing
   - Use when: action has significant consequences (sending email to 10,000 users,
     deleting database records, making financial transactions)

2. Review and Edit:
   - AI generates output → human reviews → edits if needed → confirmed output used
   - Use when: output quality is critical but AI does most of the work
   - Example: AI writes contract draft → lawyer reviews and edits → contract sent

deleting database records, making financial transactions)

2. Review and Edit:
   - AI generates output → human reviews → edits if needed → confirmed output used
   - Use when: output quality is critical but AI does most of the work
   - Example: AI writes contract draft → lawyer reviews and edits → contract sent

3. Exception Handling:
   - AI handles routine cases autonomously → escalates edge cases to humans
   - Use when: most cases are routine but some require human judgment
   - Example: AI handles standard customer queries → escalates complex complaints

4. Feedback Loop:
   - Human rates or corrects AI outputs → feedback used to improve system
   - Use when: continuous improvement of AI quality is needed
   - Implementation: thumbs up/down, star rating, manual correction stored as training data

5. Active Monitoring:
   - Human monitors AI activity in real-time without blocking it
   - Can intervene if AI is going off-track
   - Use when: AI acts autonomously but human needs situational awareness

HUMAN-IN-THE-LOOP IN LANGGRAPH:
LangGraph has built-in support for human-in-the-loop:
- interrupt_before: pause graph execution before a specific node
- interrupt_after: pause graph execution after a specific node
- graph.invoke() with thread_id: resume execution after human input
- Checkpoint: graph state saved at interrupt point, resumed after approval

WHEN TO ADD HUMAN-IN-THE-LOOP:

Mandatory:
- AI taking irreversible actions (deleting data, sending mass communications)
- Financial decisions above a threshold
- Medical or legal advice being acted upon
- Agent has access to sensitive external systems (email, CRM, payment)
- Regulated domains (healthcare, finance, legal)

Recommended:
- First N runs of a new agent in production
- When agent confidence score is below threshold
- When action affects more than N users or records
- When agent encounters an edge case it wasn't designed for

Not needed:
- Internal data retrieval (no action taken)
- Read-only AI responses (chatbot answering questions)
- Low-stakes automated tasks (categorizing internal tickets)

HITL DESIGN PRINCIPLES:
1. Make the AI's reasoning visible — show WHY the AI wants to take this action
2. Make approval frictionless — one click approve/reject, not complex forms

An architecture pattern is a proven, reusable solution to a commonly occurring problem in software architecture. It defines the overall structural organization of a software system — how components are divided, how they communicate, and how responsibilities are distributed.

Architecture patterns operate at the HLD level. They are chosen BEFORE any code is written and have long-lasting impact on the system's scalability, maintainability, deployment strategy, and team structure.

Choosing the wrong architecture pattern is extremely costly to fix later. The agent must recommend the right pattern based on project signals, scale, team size, and domain.

Six primary architecture patterns:
1. Monolithic
2. Microservices
3. Event-Driven
4. Serverless
5. SOA (Service-Oriented Architecture)
6. Layered / N-Tier

These patterns are not mutually exclusive. Real systems often combine multiple patterns. For example: Microservices architecture where individual services are internally Layered, and services communicate through an Event-Driven message bus.

MASTER DECISION RULE:
- Small project, single team, fast MVP → Monolithic
- Large system, multiple teams, independent scaling → Microservices
- Async processing, decoupled reactions to events → Event-Driven (often combined with Microservices)
- Unpredictable traffic, cost-per-execution model → Serverless
- Enterprise with existing legacy systems and SOA governance → SOA
- Traditional web apps, internal tools, clear separation of concerns → Layered N-Tier

DEFINITION:
A monolithic architecture is a single deployable unit where all components of the application — UI, business logic, data access layer — are packaged and deployed together as one application.

Everything lives in one codebase, one process, one deployment artifact.

STRUCTURE:
[Client] → [Monolithic Application (Presentation + Business Logic + Data Access)] → [Single Database]

REAL WORLD EXAMPLES:
- Early versions of Airbnb, Uber, Twitter — all started monolithic
- Most Django and Rails applications by default
- Internal company tools and admin dashboards

ADVANTAGES:
1. Simple to develop — one codebase, one repo, no network calls between components
2. Simple to test — end-to-end testing in one process
3. Simple to deploy — one artifact, one server
4. Low operational overhead — no service discovery, no distributed tracing needed
5. Fast development velocity early on — no inter-service contracts to maintain
6. Easy debugging — single process, single log stream

DISADVANTAGES:
1. Scaling problem — must scale the entire app even if only one feature is under load
2. Deployment risk — any change requires redeployment of the entire system
3. Technology lock-in — entire app must use the same language and framework
4. Growing complexity — codebase becomes a "big ball of mud" over time
5. Team bottleneck — multiple teams working on one codebase causes merge conflicts
6. Long build and deploy times as codebase grows
7. Single point of failure — one bug can crash the entire system

WHEN TO USE MONOLITHIC:
- MVP or prototype with tight deadline (under 2 months)
- Team size is 1-3 developers
- Project scope is well-defined and limited
- Expected user base under 10,000 users initially
- Internal tool or admin dashboard
- No requirement for independent scaling of components
- Early-stage startup validating product-market fit

WHEN TO AVOID MONOLITHIC:
- Multiple teams working in parallel on different features
- Different components have drastically different scaling needs
- System needs 99.99% uptime (monolith = single point of failure)
- Project is expected to grow significantly in scope

MIGRATION PATH:
Monolithic → Microservices (Strangler Fig Pattern)
→ Identify bounded contexts inside the monolith
→ Extract one service at a time
→ Route traffic to new service via API Gateway
→ Decommission extracted part from monolith
→ Repeat until fully migrated

- "todo app" → Monolithic, Django or FastAPI single app
- "internal HR tool" → Monolithic acceptable
- "MVP for startup, launch in 6 weeks" → Monolithic first, plan migration path
- "food delivery app" → Do NOT use Monolithic, too many independent concerns
- "e-commerce with 100k users" → Do NOT use Monolithic

DEFINITION:
Microservices architecture structures an application as a collection of small, independently deployable services. Each service owns a specific business capability, runs in its own process, communicates over a network (HTTP/REST or messaging), and has its own database.

Core principle: Each service is independently deployable, scalable, and replaceable.

STRUCTURE:
[Client]
   ↓
[API Gateway] — single entry point, routes to services
   ↓
[Auth Service] [User Service] [Order Service] [Payment Service] [Notification Service]
   ↓               ↓               ↓               ↓                   ↓
[Auth DB]      [User DB]      [Order DB]      [Payment DB]      [Notification DB]

Each service has its own database — this is called Database per Service pattern.
Services communicate via:
- Synchronous: REST (HTTP) or gRPC
- Asynchronous: Message queues (Kafka, RabbitMQ)

REAL WORLD EXAMPLES:
- Netflix — 700+ microservices
- Amazon — "two-pizza team" rule, each team owns one service
- Uber — split monolith into microservices when scaling became critical

2. Independent deployment — deploy one service without touching others
3. Technology diversity — each service can use different language/framework/DB
4. Fault isolation — failure in one service does not crash the entire system

4. Operational overhead — requires container orchestration (Kubernetes), service mesh
5. Distributed tracing — debugging requires tracing requests across multiple services
6. Testing complexity — integration testing requires running multiple services
7. Higher infrastructure cost — multiple databases, containers, load balancers

CORE SUPPORTING CONCEPTS FOR MICROSERVICES:
- API Gateway — single entry point, authentication, rate limiting, routing
- Service Discovery — Consul, Kubernetes DNS
- Circuit Breaker — prevents cascade failures (Hystrix, Resilience4j)
- Distributed Tracing — trace a request across services (Jaeger, Zipkin, Langfuse for AI)
- Saga Pattern — manage distributed transactions across services
- Database per Service — each service owns its data, no shared DB

WHEN TO USE MICROSERVICES:
- Large team (5+ developers) with separate sub-teams per domain

- System requires 99.99% uptime with zero-downtime deployments
- Different services need different technology stacks
- System is expected to grow to millions of users
- Organization follows domain-driven design (DDD)
- CI/CD pipeline where teams deploy independently

WHEN NOT TO USE MICROSERVICES:
- Small team (under 4 developers) — operational overhead outweighs benefits
- MVP or early-stage product — too much complexity for unknown requirements
- Simple CRUD application — no benefit from service separation
- Team lacks DevOps/Kubernetes expertise

DEFINITION:
Event-Driven Architecture (EDA) is a pattern where components communicate by producing and consuming events. An event is a record that something happened (e.g., "OrderPlaced", "PaymentCompleted", "UserRegistered"). Producers emit events without knowing who will consume them. Consumers react to events independently.

Core principle: Loose coupling through asynchronous event-based communication.

STRUCTURE:
[Producer Service] → publishes event → [Message Broker (Kafka/RabbitMQ)] → delivers event → [Consumer Service 1]
                                                                           → delivers event → [Consumer Service 2]
                                                                           → delivers event → [Consumer Service 3]

COMPONENTS:
- Event Producer — the service that generates and publishes events
- Event Broker — the message bus that stores and routes events (Kafka, RabbitMQ, AWS SNS/SQS)
- Event Consumer — the service that subscribes to and processes events
- Event Schema — the structure of the event payload (usually JSON)

TYPES OF EVENT-DRIVEN PATTERNS:
1. Pub/Sub (Publish-Subscribe) — one producer, many consumers (Kafka topics)
2. Event Streaming — continuous stream of events, consumers can replay (Kafka)
3. Message Queue — point-to-point, one consumer per message (RabbitMQ)
4. Event Sourcing — store all state changes as events, reconstruct state by replaying events
5. CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) — separate read and write models, events sync them

REAL WORLD EXAMPLES:
- Uber — "trip started" event triggers driver tracking, billing, notifications simultaneously
- Amazon — "order placed" event triggers inventory check, payment processing, shipping simultaneously
- LinkedIn — "connection request" event triggers notification, feed update, email simultaneously

ADVANTAGES:
1. Loose coupling — producer and consumer don't know about each other
2. Scalability — consumers can scale independently based on event volume
3. Resilience — if a consumer is down, events queue up and are processed when it recovers
4. Parallel processing — multiple consumers process the same event simultaneously
5. Extensibility — add new consumers without changing the producer
6. Audit trail — event log serves as complete history of everything that happened
7. Replay capability — reprocess historical events (Kafka retains events)

DISADVANTAGES:

2. Complexity in debugging — tracing an event through multiple consumers is hard
3. Event schema management — changing event schema breaks consumers
4. Ordering guarantees — ensuring events are processed in order is non-trivial
5. Duplicate event handling — consumers must be idempotent (handle same event twice safely)
6. Infrastructure dependency — requires a reliable message broker

WHEN TO USE EVENT-DRIVEN:
- Multiple services need to react to the same action (fan-out)
- Processing can be asynchronous (notifications, emails, analytics)
- Need to decouple services that would otherwise have direct dependencies
- High write throughput where synchronous processing would be a bottleneck
- Need audit trail or event replay capability
- Workflows where steps happen in sequence but not necessarily immediately

WHEN NOT TO USE EVENT-DRIVEN:
- When immediate response is required (user is waiting for synchronous result)
- Simple apps where direct service calls are sufficient
- Team unfamiliar with async programming and message brokers
- Small system where the overhead of a message broker is not justified

DEFINITION:
Serverless architecture is a model where the cloud provider manages the infrastructure, and developers deploy individual functions that are triggered by events. There are no servers to provision or manage. You pay only for the compute time your functions actually use.

Also called FaaS — Function as a Service.

Core principle: No infrastructure management. Code runs in stateless functions triggered by events.

STRUCTURE:
[Trigger (HTTP request / Event / Schedule / Queue)] → [Function (Lambda / Cloud Function)] → [External Services (DB, Storage, API)]

PROVIDERS:
- AWS Lambda — most mature, widest ecosystem
- Google Cloud Functions
- Azure Functions
- Cloudflare Workers — edge serverless, ultra low latency
- Vercel Functions — frontend-focused serverless

ADVANTAGES:
1. No infrastructure management — no servers, no patching, no scaling configuration
2. Auto-scaling — scales from 0 to millions of requests automatically
3. Cost efficiency — pay per invocation, not for idle servers
4. Fast time to market — focus on code, not infrastructure
5. Inherent high availability — cloud provider handles redundancy
6. Easy to deploy — just push the function, cloud handles the rest

5. Debugging difficulty — distributed, ephemeral environment hard to debug locally
6. Limited local testing — cloud environment differences from local setup
7. Cost unpredictability at very high scale — can become expensive

WHEN TO USE SERVERLESS:
- Event-triggered background tasks (image resizing, file processing, email sending)
- APIs with highly unpredictable or spiky traffic

- Webhook handlers

DEFINITION:
Service-Oriented Architecture is an enterprise architecture pattern where software components (services) are designed to be reused across different applications within an organization. DEFINITION:
Layered architecture (also called N-Tier architecture) organizes the system into horizontal layers, where each layer has a specific responsibility and only communicates with the layer directly above or below it.

The most common variant is the 3-Tier architecture:
1. Presentation Layer (UI) — what the user sees and interacts with
2. Business Logic Layer (Application/Service) — rules, processing, decisions
3. Data Layer (Repository/DB) — storage and retrieval of data

Extended variants:
- 4-Tier: Presentation → API → Business Logic → Data
- N-Tier: Any number of layers with clear separation

Core principle: Separation of concerns — each layer has one job and communicates only through defined interfaces.

STRUCTURE (3-Tier):
[Presentation Layer]  → React, Angular, mobile app, HTML templates
        ↓
[Business Logic Layer] → FastAPI, Django, Express.js, Spring Boot
        ↓
[Data Access Layer]   → Repository classes, ORM (SQLAlchemy, Django ORM)
        ↓
[Database]            → PostgreSQL, MySQL, MongoDB

ADVANTAGES:
1. Clear separation of concerns — each layer has one responsibility
2. Easy to understand — most developers are familiar with this pattern
3. Testability — each layer can be unit tested independently
4. Maintainability — change one layer without breaking others (if interfaces are stable)
5. Security — can enforce security at each layer boundary
6. Reusability — business logic layer can serve multiple presentation layers (web + mobile)

HLD building blocks are the fundamental infrastructure components that are added to a system design to solve specific scalability, performance, reliability, and decoupling problems. They are the toolkit of a system architect.

Every non-trivial system will use some combination of these building blocks. The agent must know WHEN to add each building block based on project signals — not ask the developer which ones to use.

Four core HLD building blocks:
1. Load Balancing — distribute traffic across multiple servers
2. Caching — reduce latency and DB load by storing frequently accessed data
3. Message Queues — decouple services and enable async processing
4. DB Sharding — scale databases horizontally across multiple machines

Additional building blocks always considered in HLD:
5. API Gateway — single entry point for all client requests
6. CDN (Content Delivery Network) — serve static assets from edge locations
7. Rate Limiting — protect services from abuse and overload
8. Service Discovery — allow services to find each other dynamically
9. Circuit Breaker — prevent cascade failures in distributed systems
10. Replication — maintain copies of data for availability and read scaling

MASTER RULE:
Every building block solves a specific problem. The agent must identify the problem first, then recommend the building block — not the other way around.

DEFINITION:
A load balancer is a component that distributes incoming network traffic across multiple servers (instances) to ensure no single server is overwhelmed. It improves availability, scalability, and fault tolerance.

Core principle: Distribute traffic → prevent single server overload → improve reliability.

STRUCTURE:
[Clients] → [Load Balancer] → [Server 1]
                           → [Server 2]
                           → [Server 3]

If Server 1 goes down, load balancer automatically routes traffic to Server 2 and 3.

TYPES OF LOAD BALANCERS:

1. Layer 4 Load Balancer (Transport Layer):
   - Routes based on IP address and TCP/UDP port
   - Does not inspect packet content
   - Faster, lower overhead
   - Used for: high-throughput, low-latency raw traffic distribution
   - Example: AWS NLB (Network Load Balancer)

2. Layer 7 Load Balancer (Application Layer):
   - Routes based on HTTP content (URL path, headers, cookies)
   - Can route /api requests to API servers and /static to CDN
   - More intelligent routing, slightly more overhead
   - Used for: HTTP traffic, microservices routing
   - Example: AWS ALB (Application Load Balancer), Nginx, HAProxy

LOAD BALANCING ALGORITHMS:

1. Round Robin — requests distributed to servers in rotation (1→2→3→1→2→3)
   Use when: all servers have equal capacity

2. Weighted Round Robin — servers with more capacity get more requests
   Use when: servers have different hardware specs

3. Least Connections — new request goes to server with fewest active connections
   Use when: requests have variable processing time

3. Least Connections — new request goes to server with fewest active connections

4. IP Hash — same client IP always goes to same server (session stickiness)
   Use when: stateful applications where session must persist on one server

5. Random — requests randomly distributed
   Use when: simple, low-traffic systems

6. Least Response Time — requests go to server with lowest latency
   Use when: latency-sensitive applications

HEALTH CHECKS:
Load balancers continuously ping servers. If a server fails health check, it is removed from the pool automatically. This is the primary mechanism for high availability.

WHEN TO ADD A LOAD BALANCER:
- System must handle more than what one server can process
- High availability is required (99.9%+ uptime)
- Horizontal scaling is planned (adding more servers)
- Zero-downtime deployments needed (route traffic away from server being deployed)
- Any production system exposed to the internet

WHEN LOAD BALANCER IS NOT NEEDED:
- Development / local environment
- Single-server internal tool
- Prototype with very low traffic

LOAD BALANCER IN MICROSERVICES:
- External Load Balancer: routes client traffic to API Gateway or service instances
- Internal Load Balancer / Service Mesh: routes traffic between microservices internally
- Examples: Nginx, HAProxy, AWS ALB, Kubernetes Ingress, Istio (service mesh)

DEFINITION:
Caching is the process of storing copies of frequently accessed data in a fast-access storage layer (cache) so that future requests for that data are served faster, without hitting the slower primary data source (database or external API).

Core principle: Store results of expensive operations → serve them faster on repeated requests → reduce DB load.

The cache sits between the application and the database. When data is requested:
1. Check cache first (cache hit) → return immediately
2. If not in cache (cache miss) → fetch from DB → store in cache → return

CACHE HIT RATE: percentage of requests served from cache. Higher is better. Target 80-95% for effective caching.

CACHING LAYERS IN A SYSTEM:

1. Client-Side Cache (Browser Cache)
   - Stored in the user's browser
   - HTTP headers control caching (Cache-Control, ETag, Expires)
   - Used for: static assets (JS, CSS, images), API responses that rarely change
   - TTL: hours to days

2. CDN Cache (Edge Cache)
   - Stored at CDN edge servers close to users geographically
   - Used for: static files, images, videos
   - Reduces latency globally, reduces origin server load
   - Examples: Cloudflare, AWS CloudFront, Fastly

3. Application-Level Cache (In-Process Cache)
   - Stored in application memory (in-process)
   - Fastest possible access (nanoseconds)
   - Lost when application restarts
   - Used for: small lookup tables, configuration data
   - Examples: Python dict, LRU cache decorator

Examples: Cloudflare, AWS CloudFront, Fastly

3. Application-Level Cache (In-Process Cache)
   - Stored in application memory (in-process)
   - Fastest possible access (nanoseconds)
   - Lost when application restarts
   - Used for: small lookup tables, configuration data
   - Examples: Python dict, LRU cache decorator

4. Distributed Cache (External Cache)
   - Stored in a dedicated cache server, shared across all application instances
   - Persists across application restarts (if configured)
   - Used for: session data, user data, API responses, computed results
   - Examples: Redis, Memcached
   - Redis is the industry standard — supports strings, lists, sets, sorted sets, hashes, pub/sub

REDIS — THE STANDARD CACHING SOLUTION:
Redis is an in-memory data structure store used as a cache, message broker, and session store.
- Speed: sub-millisecond response times
- Data structures: String, Hash, List, Set, Sorted Set, Bitmap, HyperLogLog
- Persistence: can persist to disk (RDB snapshots, AOF logs)
- TTL: set expiry time on any key
- Pub/Sub: can be used as lightweight message broker
- Atomic operations: INCR, DECR, SETNX — used for rate limiting, distributed locks

CACHING STRATEGIES:

1. Cache-Aside (Lazy Loading) — most common
   - Application checks cache first
   - On miss: fetch from DB, store in cache, return
   - Pros: only requested data is cached, resilient to cache failure
   - Cons: first request always slower (cache miss), data can be stale
   - Use when: read-heavy workload, data not always needed

side (Lazy Loading) — most common
   - Application checks cache first
   - On miss: fetch from DB, store in cache, return
   - Pros: only requested data is cached, resilient to cache failure
   - Cons: first request always slower (cache miss), data can be stale
   - Use when: read-heavy workload, data not always needed

2. Write-Through
   - Write to cache and DB simultaneously on every write
   - Cache is always up-to-date
   - Pros: no stale data
   - Cons: write latency increases, caches data that may never be read
   - Use when: data consistency is critical, read after write is common

3. Write-Behind (Write-Back)
   - Write to cache immediately, write to DB asynchronously later
   - Pros: very fast writes
   - Cons: risk of data loss if cache fails before DB write
   - Use when: high write throughput, some data loss is acceptable

4. Read-Through
   - Cache sits in front of DB, handles miss automatically
   - Application only talks to cache
   - Pros: simpler application logic
   - Cons: first request always misses
   - Use when: cache layer manages its own population logic

5. Refresh-Ahead
   - Cache proactively refreshes data before it expires
   - Pros: no cache miss latency for popular data
   - Cons: may cache data that is no longer needed
   - Use when: predictable access patterns, low tolerance for cache miss latency

CACHE INVALIDATION — THE HARDEST PROBLEM:
"There are only two hard things in Computer Science: cache invalidation and naming things."

   - Cons: may cache data that is no longer needed

CACHE INVALIDATION — THE HARDEST PROBLEM:
"There are only two hard things in Computer Science: cache invalidation and naming things."

Invalidation strategies:
- TTL (Time-To-Live): set expiry time, cache auto-expires. Simple but may serve stale data until TTL expires.
- Event-Based Invalidation: when data changes in DB, explicitly delete or update cache entry. More complex but always consistent.
- Cache Versioning: change cache key when data changes (e.g., user:123:v2). Old key auto-expires.

CACHE KEY DESIGN:
- Format: entity:id:field (e.g., user:123:profile, order:456:status)
- Include version if needed: user:123:v2
- For list results: users:page:1:limit:20
- For AI: prompt_hash:{hash_of_prompt} — semantic caching

WHAT TO CACHE:
- Database query results that are read frequently and change rarely
- Computed/aggregated results (totals, statistics, leaderboards)
- Session data and authentication tokens
- External API responses (weather, exchange rates)
- Static configuration data
- AI model responses for identical prompts (semantic caching)

WHAT NOT TO CACHE:
- Highly personalized data that is unique per request
- Rapidly changing data where staleness is unacceptable
- Sensitive financial data that must always be real-time
- Data that is rarely read (no benefit from caching)

DEFINITION:
A message queue is a component that enables asynchronous communication between services by storing messages in a queue until the receiving service is ready to process them. The producer sends a message to the queue and continues without waiting for the consumer to process it.

Core principle: Decouple producer and consumer → enable async processing → improve resilience and scalability.

STRUCTURE:
[Producer Service] → [Message Queue] → [Consumer Service]
                    (stores messages)   (processes when ready)

Producer and consumer never communicate directly. The queue acts as a buffer.

KEY CONCEPTS:

Producer: Service that creates and sends messages to the queue
Consumer: Service that reads and processes messages from the queue
Message: The data payload sent (usually JSON)
Queue: The buffer that stores messages until consumed
Topic: A named channel (in Kafka) — producers publish to topics, consumers subscribe
Acknowledgement (ACK): Consumer confirms message was processed successfully
Dead Letter Queue (DLQ): Where failed messages go after max retry attempts
Consumer Group: Multiple consumers sharing the work of processing a queue (parallelism)

TWO MAIN MESSAGE QUEUE TOOLS:

— producers publish to topics, consumers subscribe
Acknowledgement (ACK): Consumer confirms message was processed successfully
Dead Letter Queue (DLQ): Where failed messages go after max retry attempts
Consumer Group: Multiple consumers sharing the work of processing a queue (parallelism)

TWO MAIN MESSAGE QUEUE TOOLS:

1. KAFKA (Apache Kafka):
   - Distributed event streaming platform
   - Stores events as an immutable log — consumers can replay events
   - Extremely high throughput (millions of messages per second)
   - Events are retained for a configurable period (days/weeks)
   - Multiple consumer groups can read the same topic independently
   - Best for: event streaming, audit logs, real-time analytics, high-volume event pipelines
   - Concepts: Topics, Partitions, Offsets, Consumer Groups, Brokers, Zookeeper/KRaft
   - Use when: high throughput, event replay needed, multiple consumers of same event

2. RABBITMQ:
   - Traditional message broker
   - Supports multiple messaging patterns: point-to-point, pub/sub, routing, topics
   - Messages are consumed and deleted (not retained like Kafka)
   - Better for task queues where each message is processed by exactly one worker
   - Supports complex routing with exchanges and binding keys
   - Best for: task queues, job distribution, RPC over messaging
   - Use when: task distribution, routing complexity, lower throughput needs

MESSAGING PATTERNS:

1. Point-to-Point (Queue):
   - One producer → one consumer per message
   - Message deleted after consumption
   - Use for: task distribution, job queues
   - Example: Order placed → payment processing task queue

2. Publish-Subscribe (Pub/Sub):
   - One producer → multiple consumers, all receive the same message
   - Use for: broadcasting events to multiple interested services
   - Example: "OrderPlaced" → NotificationService + InventoryService + AnalyticsService all consume

3. Request-Reply:
   - Producer sends message and waits for reply on a reply queue
   - Async version of synchronous RPC
   - Use for: inter-service communication where response is needed but latency flexibility exists

4. Dead Letter Queue (DLQ):
   - Messages that fail processing after N retries go to DLQ
   - Allows investigation without blocking the main queue
   - Every production queue should have a DLQ configured

5. Priority Queue:
   - Messages assigned priority levels, higher priority processed first
   - Use for: urgent task processing (premium user requests before free tier)

MESSAGE QUEUE BENEFITS IN DETAIL:

1. Decoupling: Producer doesn't need to know about consumers. Add new consumers without changing producer.

2. Load Leveling: Queue absorbs traffic spikes. Producer can burst, consumer processes at steady rate.
   Example: Black Friday order spike → orders go to queue → payment service processes at its own pace

3. Resilience: If consumer goes down, messages wait in queue. No data loss.

4. Scalability: Add more consumers to process queue faster during high load.

5. Retry Logic: Failed messages automatically retried before going to DLQ.

DEFINITION:
Database sharding is the process of partitioning a large database horizontally into smaller, faster, more manageable pieces called shards. Each shard is a separate database instance that holds a subset of the total data. Together, all shards hold the complete dataset.

Core principle: Split data across multiple DB instances → each instance handles a fraction of the load → system scales horizontally.

STRUCTURE:
[Application]
     ↓
[Shard Router / Proxy]
     ↓            ↓            ↓
[Shard 1]    [Shard 2]    [Shard 3]
Users A-M    Users N-T    Users U-Z

Each shard is an independent PostgreSQL (or MySQL, MongoDB) instance.

KEY CONCEPTS:

Shard Key: The field used to determine which shard a record belongs to.
Choosing the right shard key is the most critical sharding decision.
Good shard key: evenly distributes data and queries across shards, avoids hotspots.

Shard Router: The component that determines which shard a query goes to based on the shard key.

Hotspot: When one shard receives disproportionately more traffic than others. Bad shard key causes hotspots.

Rebalancing: When shards become uneven, data must be moved between shards. Operationally complex.

SHARDING STRATEGIES:

1. Range-Based Sharding:
   - Data divided by value ranges of the shard key
   - Example: User ID 1-1M → Shard 1, 1M-2M → Shard 2, 2M-3M → Shard 3
   - Pros: Simple, range queries efficient
   - Cons: Uneven distribution if data is not uniformly distributed (hotspot risk)
   - Use when: time-series data (date as shard key), ordered data

rding:
   - Data divided by value ranges of the shard key
   - Example: User ID 1-1M → Shard 1, 1M-2M → Shard 2, 2M-3M → Shard 3
   - Pros: Simple, range queries efficient
   - Cons: Uneven distribution if data is not uniformly distributed (hotspot risk)
   - Use when: time-series data (date as shard key), ordered data

2. Hash-Based Sharding:
   - Shard key is hashed, hash determines the shard
   - Example: shard = hash(user_id) % number_of_shards
   - Pros: Even distribution, no hotspots
   - Cons: Range queries inefficient (data scattered), adding shards requires rehashing
   - Use when: uniform random access, no range query requirements

3. Directory-Based Sharding:
   - A lookup table (directory) maps each shard key to its shard
   - Pros: Flexible, easy to rebalance by updating directory
   - Cons: Directory becomes a bottleneck and single point of failure
   - Use when: complex sharding logic, need flexible reassignment

4. Geographic Sharding:
   - Data partitioned by geography (region/country)
   - Example: US users → US shard, EU users → EU shard, APAC → APAC shard
   - Pros: Data locality (low latency), compliance (GDPR data residency)
   - Cons: Uneven if regions have different user volumes
   - Use when: global systems with data residency requirements

   - Data partitioned by geography (region/country)

   - Pros: Data locality (low latency), compliance (GDPR data residency)
   - Cons: Uneven if regions have different user volumes
   - Use when: global systems with data residency requirements

CHOOSING THE RIGHT SHARD KEY:
Good shard key criteria:
- High cardinality → many possible values (not boolean)
- Even distribution → data spreads evenly across shards
- Query alignment → most queries filter by this key (avoids cross-shard queries)
- Immutable → shard key should not change (changing it requires moving data)

Common shard keys:
- User ID → for user-centric data
- Geographic region → for global systems
- Tenant ID → for multi-tenant SaaS
- Date/time → for time-series data
- Order ID → for order data

CROSS-SHARD QUERIES (the main problem):
- Queries that span multiple shards are expensive
- Must query all shards and merge results at application level
- Solution: design schema and shard key so most queries hit one shard
- Avoid JOINs across shards — keep related data on the same shard

SHARDING vs OTHER SCALING APPROACHES:

HARD QUERIES (the main problem):
- Queries that span multiple shards are expensive
- Must query all shards and merge results at application level
- Solution: design schema and shard key so most queries hit one shard
- Avoid JOINs across shards — keep related data on the same shard

SHARDING vs OTHER SCALING APPROACHES:

Approach            | What it solves              | When to use
--------------------|-----------------------------|-----------------------------------------
Vertical Scaling    | Single DB too slow          | First step — upgrade hardware
Read Replicas       | Too many read queries       | Read-heavy workload, before sharding
Caching (Redis)     | Repeated read queries       | Most systems, before DB scaling
Connection Pooling  | Too many DB connections     | High concurrency (PgBouncer)
Partitioning        | Large table in single DB    | When table has billions of rows
Sharding            | Single DB can't handle load | Last resort for extreme scale

RULE: Sharding is the last resort. Before sharding:
1. Add caching (Redis) — eliminates most read load
2. Add read replicas — distribute read queries
3. Optimize queries — indexes, query rewriting
4. Vertical scale — upgrade DB hardware
5. Table partitioning — within single DB instance
6. Then shard — only when all above are insufficient

WHEN TO ADD SHARDING TO HLD:
- Database is projected to exceed 1-2 TB of data
- Write throughput exceeds what one DB instance can handle (typically > 10,000 writes/second)
- Single DB instance cannot be vertically scaled further
- System serves 10M+ users
- Time-series data growing indefinitely (IoT, analytics, logs)

WHEN NOT TO SHARD:
- System has fewer than 1 million users
- DB size is under 500 GB
- Read replicas + caching have not been tried yet
- Team lacks expertise to manage distributed DB
- MVP or early-stage product — premature optimization

SHARDING IN NOSQL vs SQL:
- MongoDB: built-in sharding support, easier to shard than SQL
- Cassandra: sharding is native (called partitioning), uses consistent hashing
- PostgreSQL: manual sharding (Citus extension for automatic sharding)
- MySQL: manual sharding at application level

DECISION RULE — INFER FROM PROJECT:
- "social media platform with 100M users" → sharding needed for user data
- "IoT sensor data" → time-based sharding mandatory
- "global e-commerce with 50M orders/day" → order data sharding needed
- "startup with 10,000 users" → DO NOT shard, use caching + read replicas
- "food delivery app MVP" → DO NOT shard, premature

API GATEWAY:
Definition: Single entry point for all client requests. Routes requests to appropriate services, handles cross-cutting concerns.
Responsibilities: Authentication, rate limiting, SSL termination, request routing, response transformation, logging.
Tools: AWS API Gateway, Kong, Nginx, Traefik, FastAPI (as lightweight gateway)
When to add: Any microservices system, any public API, any system where auth must be centralized.
Decision rule: "microservices" → API Gateway mandatory. "monolith" → optional.

CDN (Content Delivery Network):
Definition: Network of geographically distributed servers that cache and serve static content from locations close to users.
Used for: Static files (JS, CSS, images, videos), API responses that don't change per user.
Tools: Cloudflare, AWS CloudFront, Fastly, Akamai.
Benefits: Reduced latency globally, reduced origin server load, DDoS protection.
When to add: Any system with global users, any media-heavy application, any system serving static assets.
Decision rule: "global users" or "images/videos/static files" → CDN mandatory.

RATE LIMITING:
Definition: Controlling the number of requests a client can make in a given time period.
Algorithms: Token Bucket, Leaky Bucket, Fixed Window Counter, Sliding Window Log.
Implementation: Redis-based counters (INCR with TTL), API Gateway rate limiting.
When to add: Any public API, any AI API to control LLM costs, any system vulnerable to abuse.
Decision rule: "public API" or "LLM-powered app" → Rate limiting mandatory.

SERVICE DISCOVERY:
Definition: Mechanism by which services in a microservices system find each other's network location dynamically.
Tools: Consul, Kubernetes DNS, Eureka, AWS Cloud Map.
When to add: Any microservices system where services scale dynamically.
Decision rule: "microservices on Kubernetes" → Service discovery handled by Kubernetes DNS automatically.

CIRCUIT BREAKER:
Definition: Automatically stops calling a failing service and returns a fallback response, preventing cascade failures.
States: Closed (normal), Open (failing, stop calls), Half-Open (testing recovery).
Tools: Resilience4j (Java), Hystrix, Polly (.NET), custom implementation in Python.
When to add: Any microservices system, any system with external API calls that can fail.
Decision rule: "microservices" or "external API dependency" → Circuit Breaker recommended.

DATABASE REPLICATION:
Definition: Maintaining copies of the database across multiple instances.
Types:
- Primary-Replica (Master-Slave): One primary handles writes, replicas handle reads.
- Multi-Primary: Multiple primaries handle writes (complex, conflict resolution needed).
Benefits: Read scalability, high availability, failover.
When to add: Any system with read-heavy workload, any system requiring high availability.
Decision rule: Before sharding, always try read replicas first. "high availability" → replication mandatory.

CONNECTION POOLING:
Definition: Maintaining a pool of reusable database connections to avoid the overhead of creating new connections.
Tools: PgBouncer (PostgreSQL), SQLAlchemy connection pool, asyncpg pool.
When to add: Any production PostgreSQL system with high concurrency.
Decision rule: "FastAPI + PostgreSQL" → PgBouncer or SQLAlchemy pool always.

A design strategy is the approach or methodology used to plan and structure a software system before and during development. It defines the ORDER and DIRECTION in which a system is broken down and built.

Design strategies are not the same as architecture patterns. Architecture patterns define the STRUCTURE of the system (what it looks like). Design strategies define the PROCESS of designing that structure (how you think about it).

Three primary design strategies exist:
1. Top-Down Design
2. Bottom-Up Design
3. Modular Design

In real projects, these strategies are often combined. Understanding which strategy to lead with determines how effectively the team can plan, divide work, and deliver.

DECISION RULE:
- Most enterprise and AI projects → start Top-Down, implement Bottom-Up
- Library or SDK development → Bottom-Up
- Large team with parallel workstreams → Modular
- Solo developer building MVP → Modular with Bottom-Up execution

DEFINITION:
Top-Down design starts with the complete system as a whole and progressively breaks it down into smaller sub-systems, components, and modules. You define the big picture first, then drill into details.

The process:
1. Define the overall system goal
2. Identify major sub-systems
3. Break each sub-system into components
4. Break components into functions/modules
5. Define data flow between all parts
6. Then implement

ANALOGY:
Think of building a city. You first decide the city layout (zones: residential, commercial, industrial), then plan roads between zones, then design individual buildings, then furnish rooms inside buildings. You always start from the largest unit.

WHO USES IT:
- Architects and tech leads during HLD phase
- Teams designing enterprise systems
- Teams doing system design interviews

ADVANTAGES:
- Forces clarity on overall system before writing any code
- Makes dependencies and interfaces visible early
- Easier to communicate with stakeholders (they see the big picture)
- Reduces risk of building the wrong thing
- Enables parallel team assignments once components are defined

DISADVANTAGES:
- Risk of over-engineering early (designing things that may not be needed)
- Can be slow to start implementation — team waits for full design
- Details at lower levels may reveal flaws in the top-level design
- Requires experienced architects to do it well

WHEN TO USE TOP-DOWN:
- Building a new system from scratch with unknown scope
- Large team (5+ developers) that needs clear division of responsibilities
- Project has complex integrations between many services
- Stakeholders need architecture sign-off before development
- AI/agentic systems where agent boundaries must be clear before implementation
- Regulated domains (fintech, healthcare) where architecture must be reviewed

WHEN NOT TO USE TOP-DOWN ALONE:
- Experimental projects where requirements are unclear
- Startups iterating fast on product-market fit
- When the team needs to prove a concept before designing the full system

DECISION RULE — INFER FROM PROJECT:
- "enterprise resource planning" → Top-Down mandatory
- "AI agent system with 5 agents" → Top-Down to define agent boundaries first
- "hospital management system" → Top-Down for compliance and clarity
- "startup MVP in 2 weeks" → Top-Down too heavy, prefer Modular + Bottom-Up

DEFINITION:
Bottom-Up design starts with the smallest, most fundamental components and builds upward by composing them into larger systems. You build and validate individual pieces first, then integrate them.

The process:
1. Identify core low-level components needed
2. Build and test each component independently
3. Compose components into larger modules
4. Integrate modules into sub-systems
5. Assemble sub-systems into the full system

ANALOGY:
Think of building with LEGO. You first create individual LEGO pieces (bricks), test that each brick snaps correctly, then build small structures from bricks, then combine structures into a full model. You never see the final model until all pieces are assembled.

WHO USES IT:
- Developers during implementation phase
- Library and framework authors
- Teams working on microservices where each service is built independently
- Data engineers building ETL pipelines component by component

ADVANTAGES:
- Each component is tested and validated before integration
- Promotes reusability — components built independently can be reused elsewhere
- Works well with TDD (Test-Driven Development)
- Team can work in parallel on different components
- Easier to debug — issues isolated to individual components

DISADVANTAGES:
- Risk of building components that don't integrate well later
- Without a top-level vision, components may not align with overall goals
- Integration issues discovered late in the process
- Can lead to inconsistent interfaces between components if not coordinated

WHEN TO USE BOTTOM-UP:
- Building shared libraries, SDKs, or utility packages
- Microservices where each service is independently deployable
- Data pipelines where each transformation step is a standalone unit
- Proof of concept — validate a single component before designing the full system
- Refactoring legacy code — rewrite components one at a time

WHEN NOT TO USE BOTTOM-UP ALONE:
- When the overall system architecture is unclear
- When multiple teams need to integrate their work — interfaces must be agreed upfront
- When stakeholders need visibility into overall system design

COMBINATION RULE (most common in practice):
Top-Down for PLANNING + Bottom-Up for IMPLEMENTATION
→ Design the full system top-down (HLD)
→ Build component by component bottom-up (LLD + implementation)
→ This is the standard approach for most production systems

DECISION RULE — INFER FROM PROJECT:
- "build a reusable authentication library" → Bottom-Up
- "build individual microservices" → Bottom-Up per service
- "validate RAG retrieval before full agent" → Bottom-Up (prove retrieval works first)
- "full product with multiple teams" → Top-Down plan, Bottom-Up execution

DEFINITION:
Modular design structures the system as a collection of independent, self-contained, reusable modules. Each module has a clearly defined responsibility, a well-defined interface, and can be developed, tested, and deployed independently.

Core principle: HIGH COHESION within a module, LOW COUPLING between modules.
- High cohesion → everything inside a module is related to one responsibility
- Low coupling → modules depend on each other as little as possible

The process:
1. Identify distinct functional areas of the system
2. Define each area as an independent module with clear boundaries
3. Define the interface (API contract) each module exposes
4. Develop each module independently
5. Integrate modules through their interfaces

ANALOGY:
Think of a smartphone. The camera module, battery module, speaker module, and display module are all independent. The camera can be upgraded without touching the battery. Each module communicates through defined interfaces (connectors). This is modular design.

WHO USES IT:
- All modern software teams — modular design is the default in professional software
- Especially important in microservices, plugin architectures, and large codebases
- Frontend teams (component-based UI in React, Vue)
- Backend teams (service modules, domain modules)

KEY PROPERTIES OF A GOOD MODULE:
1. Single Responsibility — one module does one thing well
2. Well-defined interface — input/output contract is clear and stable
3. Encapsulation — internal implementation hidden from outside
4. Replaceability — can swap one module for another without breaking the system
5. Testability — can be unit tested independently

ADVANTAGES:
- Parallel development — different teams build different modules simultaneously
- Easier maintenance — fix one module without affecting others
- Reusability — modules can be reused across projects
- Scalability — scale only the modules under load, not the entire system
- Onboarding — new developers can understand and own individual modules

DISADVANTAGES:
- Requires upfront interface design — changing interfaces later is costly
- Over-modularization can add unnecessary complexity for small projects
- Inter-module communication overhead (especially in distributed systems)
- Requires discipline to maintain boundaries — teams tend to create shortcuts

In professional system design, all three strategies are used together:

PHASE 1 — PLANNING (Top-Down):
→ Define overall system, break into major components, assign responsibilities

PHASE 2 — STRUCTURING (Modular):
→ Define each component as an independent module with clear interfaces

PHASE 3 — IMPLEMENTATION (Bottom-Up):
→ Build and test each module independently, then integrate

EXAMPLE — Food Delivery App:
Top-Down:

Modular:
